structure.notes: The required Stage 0 sections and locked-parameter block are present and cluster-appropriate, and the §14 checklist is mostly actionable. However the TeX artifact uses a conjecture environment at lines 127 and 150 without defining \newtheorem{conjecture}{Conjecture} in the preamble, so the note will not compile as written; the referenced assumption table and Lean artifact directory named by the prompt are also absent in the workspace, though the TeX checklist itself is present.

novelty.notes: The derived kernel falls below the orchestrator-enforced flagship floor. The main accepted mathematics is finite convex/linear-algebra geometry of a bounded bridge fiber, a Tikhonov exact-fiber limit, a one-dimensional selector characterization, and one explicit binary witness. This is useful and plausibly publishable as a narrow technical diagnostic, but it does not prove a flagship open-regime theorem. The original unqualified one-dimensional conjecture is refuted, and the repair is a nonradial one-dimensional condition rather than a broad generic-class result.

correctness.notes: The displayed algebra for the LP interval, null-annihilator equivalence, exact-fiber Tikhonov limit, one-dimensional selector formula, and binary witness checks out. The binary witness arithmetic gives P(W=0)=7/16, P(W=1)=9/16, ell(h(t))=-1/40+t/8, and t_lambda=1/(1+lambda). The causal sharpness conclusion is explicitly conditional on the strong finite bridge-fiber sharpness assumption.

review.verbatim_critique:
The note is structurally close to Stage 0 format but has a concrete TeX break: the preamble defines definition, assumption, example, theorem, proposition, lemma, and corollary environments, but not conjecture, while the Introduction uses \begin{conjecture} at lines 127 and 150. This should be fixed before downstream stages consume the artifact. The locked-parameter block at lines 260-295 matches the Stage -1 block and correctly identifies the PartialID/exact-functional boundary convention: observed finite law P, target theta=E[Y(1)-Y(0)], finite bridge-fiber sharpness, bounding functional Theta_I(P), and quadratic selection functional. The required sections are present, and §14 is usable as a math-object checklist rather than a Lean mapping.

The decisive failure is novelty relative to the enforced flagship target. The Stage -1 proposal itself identified the routine parts as the null-annihilator criterion and exact-fiber Tikhonov limit, with the flagship being an open-class quadratic selection-range theorem. The Stage 0 note proves the routine finite convex facts, proves a one-dimensional nonradial selector formula, gives a radial counterexample to the unqualified one-dimensional claim, and constructs a small binary witness. Those are coherent, but they do not amount to a flagship theorem. Proposition 3 only gives full selector-closure equality in a one-dimensional nonradial fiber, while the original conjecture's unqualified one-dimensional statement is false and the broad generic/open-class selector-range result is not proved beyond this restricted geometry. Proposition 4 is a single explicit nondegenerate law, not a generic-class obstruction or a broad new identification regime. Conditional finite bridge-fiber sharpness is also assumed, not derived, so the causal sharpness content is pushed into an assumption. Under the orchestrator's novelty_target=flagship directive, this cannot be accepted at a lower tier.

Correctness is otherwise acceptable. Proposition 1 follows directly from the finite bridge-fiber sharpness assumption plus linear-image compactness. Proposition 2's exact-fiber Tikhonov argument is valid on the compact box: residuals converge to zero and every cluster point is the unique Q-minimum point in the exact fiber. Proposition 3's formula t_Q=Pi[-r'Qh0/(r'Qr)] is correct, and the nonradial argument is recoverable from the existence of an SPD inner product making two independent vectors orthogonal. The radial counterexample correctly shows selector collapse at t=0. For Proposition 4, the A=1 bridge equations solve to (1/5,4/5); the stated observable law gives P(W=0)=7/16 and P(W=1)=9/16; hence ell(h(t))=-1/40+t/8 and the sharp interval is [-1/40,1/10]. The diagonal objective lambda t^2+(1-t)^2 has minimizer t_lambda=1/(1+lambda), giving the stated selected ATE curve.

review.verbatim_critique:
The note is structurally close to the required Stage 0 format: all required section roles are present, assumptions are labelled, the locked-parameter block is cluster-aware, and Section 14 gives a usable math-object checklist rather than premature Lean mapping. The concrete structural defect is that the preamble defines no conjecture environment, while the Introduction uses \begin{conjecture} twice at lines 128 and 150. A pdflatex check fails at line 128 with 'Environment conjecture undefined.' This is fixable but blocks downstream use of the TeX artifact as written.

The decisive failure is novelty relative to novelty_target=flagship. The main mathematics actually delivered is finite convex geometry of the bridge fiber, a null-annihilator criterion, convergence of residual-plus-quadratic Tikhonov selectors to exact-fiber minimum-Q-norm bridges, a one-dimensional nonradial selector formula, and one explicit binary table. These are coherent diagnostics, but they are not a flagship regime-opening theorem. The broad Conjecture 1 open-class selector-range statement is not proved; the unqualified one-dimensional claim is explicitly false and is replaced by a narrower nonradial condition. Proposition 4 is a single constructed law rather than an open class of observable laws or a generic-class obstruction. The causal sharpness content is also assumed through finite bridge-fiber sharpness rather than derived. Under the floor directive, this kernel cannot be accepted below flagship; lifting it would require a new generic/open-class theorem, not a local exposition patch.

Correctness is mostly sound for Propositions 1-3. Proposition 1 follows from the finite bridge-fiber sharpness assumption and the compact convex linear-image lemma. Proposition 2's exact-fiber limit argument is valid on the compact box, and the normal-cone condition has the right sign under the displayed convention. Proposition 3's coordinate formula and radial obstruction are correct; the SPD-orthogonalization step is recoverable from linear independence of x and r. The binary witness arithmetic also checks: the A=1 bridge equations solve to (1/5,4/5), the stated law gives P(W=0)=7/16 and P(W=1)=9/16, hence ell(h(t))=-1/40+t/8 and the diagonal objective has minimizer t_lambda=1/(1+lambda). However Proposition 4 claims the witness satisfies the generic-stratum condition from Conjecture 2. That is not established and appears false: in the A=0 arm the two bridge rows are identical, P(W=0|Z=0,A=0)=P(W=0|Z=1,A=0)=1/2, so the rank drop that creates the one-dimensional fiber is destroyed by arbitrarily small perturbations of the observable law. Thus matrix rank is not constant on an open neighborhood in the ordinary law space, contrary to Assumption generic-stratum. This correctness issue further prevents acceptance, independently of the flagship novelty failure.

reason: Case 6b + regression pattern (a) — Stage 0.5 reject is conjecture-level: the flagship generic selector-range Conjecture 1 is not proved and the unqualified one-dimensional claim is explicitly false; the only derivations achieved sit at subfield tier and Proposition 4's witness has a structural rank-stability defect (A=0 duplicate rows), so re-deriving on the same .tex cannot meet the flagship novelty floor — pivot to a new angle.
proposed_action: Abandon the current eid_proximal_phase_v1 angle (flagship 'generic open-class selector-range' Conjecture 1 plus binary witness for the generic-stratum claim) and draft a new Stage -1 angle that either (i) targets a genuinely open-class / generic-stratum proximal identification theorem with a stable-rank witness, or (ii) reformulates the contribution at a tier the math can actually deliver (e.g. a sharp characterization of nonradial selector failure on an explicit open regime), so that the §1 flagship statement matches what §3-§13 can prove.
